name: Kelpie
description: The Kelpie is a shape-shifting water spirit inhabiting the lochs and pools of Scotland. It typically appears as a beautiful horse, often with a wet and glistening coat, enticing weary travelers to ride upon its back. However, once mounted, the Kelpie's skin becomes adhesive, trapping its rider. The Kelpie then plunges into the deepest part of the water, drowning its victim and devouring them, leaving only the entrails and liver to float to the surface. Kelpies are often associated with sudden and unexplained drownings, and their cries are said to forewarn of approaching storms. While usually appearing as a horse, they can also take human form, often as a handsome man, to lure unsuspecting victims closer to the water's edge. They are considered dangerous and malevolent creatures, a constant threat in the Scottish Highlands.
subtitle: Deadly shape-shifting water horse of Scotland
appearance: Typically appears as a beautiful, powerful black horse with a wet and glistening coat. Its mane is often tangled with weeds and rushes. It can also take the form of a handsome man, but may retain horse-like features, such as seaweed in its hair or hooves instead of feet. Its skin is cold and clammy to the touch.
habitat: Lochs, rivers, and pools throughout Scotland, particularly in the Highlands.
behavior: Kelpies are predatory creatures that actively lure humans to their watery graves. They are cunning and patient, waiting for the opportune moment to strike. They are often associated with storms and are said to be more active during periods of heavy rain and turbulent water. While generally malevolent, some tales suggest that Kelpies can be controlled with the correct bridle, granting the owner great power.

keyMyths.title: The Kelpie's Bridle
keyMyths.description: A blacksmith once crafted a bridle of silver and placed it on a Kelpie, forcing it to work for him for a year and a day. After its servitude, the Kelpie was freed and vanished into the loch.

keyMyths.title: The Drowning Pool
keyMyths.description: Villagers tell tales of a Kelpie luring children to a seemingly shallow pool, only for it to suddenly deepen and drown them. Their bodies were never recovered, believed to be consumed by the Kelpie.

sources.author: Katharine Briggs
